_comment: THE FUNCTION LIBRARY. Interactive features and data-capture mechanisms. Each is built once and reused. 'data' = what it captures. 'goals' = which goals it serves. 'principle' = the research-backed reason, with a source. The capture/intelligence functions are the moat: they are what a competitor with the same AI tools would not think to build.

items.name: Listening-device chatbot
items.does: Answers predictable questions instantly, captures a lead on every question it cannot answer, and logs every question as research.
items.data: Question text (intent), name, email or phone on handoff, page asked from, the misses (the exact list of things the site fails to say).
items.whenToUse: Predictable question set, one location, fixed prices: use rule-based (never invents a price, costs nothing per message, you own every word). Variable or multilingual at scale: consider AI or hybrid.
items.principle: The unanswered question is the treasure: it writes your next pages. Match the tool to query complexity rather than defaulting to AI.
items.source: Born Digital, rule-based vs AI chatbots

items.name: Product / apartment finder quiz
items.does: Walks a visitor through a few questions and recommends the right option, learning their whole situation in the act of helping.
items.data: Self-declared needs, budget, use-case, party size, timing (pure zero-party data), plus email at the results gate.
items.whenToUse: Any business where 'which one is right for me?' is real friction: apartments, supplements, service tiers, products.
items.principle: Quizzes build audience profiles ~3.8x faster than pixel tracking and average $6.40 revenue per $1 spent; quiz pages hold attention ~2.5x longer than static.

items.name: One-step / conversational lead capture
items.does: Captures a contact with the fewest fields possible, one question at a time, phone optional with a reason, and fires an instant notification.
items.data: Name, email or phone (their choice), the question or need, the page they were on.
items.whenToUse: Everywhere a visitor might want a human. Default to multi-step past ~3 fields.
items.principle: Cutting fields and asking one thing at a time lifts completion sharply; phone is the most abandonment-prone field, so make it optional and say why.
items.source: Baymard checkout usability; multi-step form studies

items.name: Booking / enquiry flow
items.does: A real booking or enquiry with live pricing and a one-step capture, no forced account, flowing into the customer database.
items.data: Dates, party size, option chosen, name, email, every funnel step (where they drop).
items.whenToUse: Hospitality, F&B, clinics, any appointment or reservation business.
items.principle: 24% of shoppers abandon when forced to register, so offer the account, never gate on it. Capture beats convert.

items.name: Exit and scroll-triggered offer
items.does: Shows a targeted offer or capture based on behavior: on the way out, or once a reader is engaged.
items.data: Email or phone, the offer claimed, the trigger and page that converted them.
items.whenToUse: Scroll/engaged trigger on content pages; exit-intent on high-value pages (pricing, checkout). Never on immediate load.
items.principle: Scroll-triggered popups convert ~5.4% vs ~3.9% for exit-intent, because they catch interest while it is still warm. Exit-intent still recovers 10-15% of leavers.

items.name: On-site A/B offer test
items.does: Runs two or more welcome offers (or CTAs) against live traffic and records which one actually wins the most contacts.
items.data: Per-variant conversion rate, which offer / headline / CTA wins, by segment.
items.whenToUse: Once there is enough traffic for significance. Test big swings on low-traffic local sites.
items.principle: Replace 'I think 15% off is good' with evidence. Predetermine sample size: 531 of 1,000 A/A tests hit '95% significance' by chance.

items.name: Exit / objection survey
items.does: Catches someone leaving or dismissing an offer and asks one positively framed question: what is holding you back today?
items.data: The real reason people do not convert (price, dates, need more info, just browsing), in their words.
items.whenToUse: On booking and pricing pages, and at offer dismissal.
items.principle: Framing matters: 'what could we do better' gets answered, 'why did you fail to convert' gets ignored. Every answer is intelligence you had none of.

items.name: Preference center / progressive profiling
items.does: Lets a returning customer declare what they care about, collected a few fields at a time across visits instead of one giant form.
items.data: Stated interests, preferences, communication channel and frequency, life-stage, all zero-party.
items.whenToUse: Any business building an owned audience. The strategic backbone of a 'mine customer data' offer.
items.principle: Progressive profiling yields 65-80% profile completeness vs 20-35% for one big form. Zero-party data is the durable asset as tracking erodes.

items.name: Confirmation-page capture
items.does: Uses the highest-trust moment, right after a booking or purchase, to ask 2-3 light questions and prompt an account or preferences.
items.data: Attribution (how did you hear about us), motivation, arrival time and preferences, and which unbuilt service they want (demand validation).
items.whenToUse: Every business with a checkout or booking confirmation. The lowest-friction capture point: the decision is already made.
items.principle: The confirmation screen is the most-opened, highest-trust, lowest-pressure moment you will ever get. Most businesses waste it on logistics.

items.name: Funnel analytics + session replay
items.does: Logs every step of a flow so you can see exactly where people drop, and watch real sessions to see why.
items.data: Step-by-step conversion and drop-off, event stream, heatmaps, recorded sessions of users who bailed.
items.whenToUse: Any site with a multi-step flow. It is the function that tells you which other functions to fix.
items.principle: Service checkouts abandon ~70%. You cannot fix what you cannot see; quantitative tells you where, session replay tells you why.

items.name: Technical SEO + AEO schema
items.does: The structured-data, sitemap, meta and speed scaffolding that makes a site indexable and quotable by AI assistants.
items.data: n/a (infrastructure). Makes the site's facts machine-readable.
items.whenToUse: Every client. The shape is standardized; only the values change per client.
items.principle: Pages with FAQ markup are ~3.2x more likely to appear in AI Overviews; clean schema earns ~2.8x higher AI citation. Only ~12% of sites use structured data, so early movers win. Front-load answers in under 30 words.

items.name: Google Business Profile + local SEO
items.does: An optimized, actively managed Google Business Profile that ranks in the map pack, plus consistent name/address/phone across the web.
items.data: Calls, direction requests, profile-driven clicks, review text and sentiment, the queries that surfaced the profile.
items.whenToUse: Every local, physical, or service-area business.
items.principle: Primary category is the #1 local-pack factor; consistent NAP makes a business ~40% more likely to appear in the local pack; Google rewards actively managed profiles over merely complete ones.

items.name: Reviews and referral pipeline
items.does: Systematically asks for reviews after good experiences and gives customers a structured way to refer others.
items.data: Review text and ratings (which feed GBP and AEO), referrer-referee links, referral source, advocate identity.
items.whenToUse: Every business with satisfied customers. Tie review requests to the post-purchase moment.
items.principle: Purchase likelihood is 270% greater with five reviews than none, and peaks at 4.0-4.7 stars (a perfect 5.0 reads as fake). 92% trust referrals from people they know.

items.name: Accounts + saved bookings
items.does: A real logged-in account with saved details and history, turning an anonymous visitor into a persistent, owned relationship.
items.data: Identity, full booking/purchase history, saved preferences, behavior tied to a known person over time.
items.whenToUse: Repeat-purchase or recurring-booking businesses. Introduce at the high-trust post-purchase moment, not as a barrier before purchase.
items.principle: Repeat customers spend 67% more; a 5% retention lift raises profit ~25%. An owned account is the difference between renting attention and owning it.
